content:
Hello all,

A friend of mine reached out to me with some questions re:  licensing and Rust code in the Linux kernel. (Specifically, whether the Rust code being added to the Linux kernel was being licensed under something other than GPL-2.0)

I did some research and found that, yes, code that is brought over from the Rust standard library has the license of "Apache-2.0 OR MIT", rather than GPL-2.0..

NOTE - there are a few other instances in rust/macros and rust/kernel - I assume that is because those are related to the standard library somehow?

However, it looks like the vast majority of other Rust code in the kernel is licensed under GPL-2.0.

My friend also asked whether any non-GPL-2.0 code affects the API or linker surface area of the kernel. My instinct on this is no, but I wanted to verify that here first (I was not able to find a definitive answer through documentation search, github search, zulip history search, etc.)

I did some research and found that, yes, code that is brought over from the Rust standard library has the license of "Apache-2.0 OR MIT", rather than GPL-2.0..

Yeah, anything from the Rust standard library should have the right license.

NOTE - there are a few other instances in rust/macros and rust/kernel - I assume that is because those are related to the standard library somehow?

For std_vendor.rs files, yes -- please see the comment at the top of each file.

For other files, it depends on the authors and what it is, i.e. there are several licenses one may use. Please see https://docs.kernel.org/process/license-rules.html.

However, it looks like the vast majority of other Rust code in the kernel is licensed under GPL-2.0.

Yes, that is intentional.

My friend also asked whether any non-GPL-2.0 code affects the API or linker surface area of the kernel. My instinct on this is no, but I wanted to verify that here first

I am not sure what you mean, but if it helps: all Rust exported symbols are exported as EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL -- please see the comment at the top of rust/exports.c (and the link above to the kernel licensing rules etc.).
A message was moved from this topic to #Help > build asahi linux kernel by Trevor Gross.
